burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT.

narrative_blocks.header: Witnessed the Brace will, decades before the Dictionary
narrative_blocks.body: On September 27, 1787, Noah Webster swore the witnesses of Henry Brace's will as Justice of the Peace in Hartford — he was 29 years old. Forty years later, in 1828, he published the American Dictionary of the English Language, assisted principally by James Gates Percival, a 6th-generation Hooker descendant.

narrative_blocks.header: Recruited a Hooker descendant for his Dictionary
narrative_blocks.body: Noah Webster recruited James Gates Percival to edit the 1828 American Dictionary. Percival — poet, geologist, Yale graduate, fluent in over twenty languages — corrected Webster's errors in scientific and European language terms. "I feel like the living tied to the dead," Percival wrote of the collaboration.

cross_connections.display_label: Hooker descendant Percival edited the 1828 Dictionary
cross_connections.description: Webster recruited him to edit the 1828 Dictionary — Percival corrected his mistakes

cross_connections.display_label: Witnessed Capt. Brace's will in 1787 — aged 29, not yet famous

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
